item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is… is this a sad ending… -without- you trying to forcefully make us feel sad?
I like this. I really like non-forced feels.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so sad, I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Might be too late to say this but a suit like that probably has multiple layers of insulation, just in case. Maybe it has warning systems for single-layer breaks, such as radiation releases.
Probably only an internal warning light or alarm, though, since anti-world wouldn't believe sentient contact to be likely. Also whatever punctures the first layer can probably puncture a second identical layer.
It's probably not a perfect fix to the sense of the plan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the ending here. They realized their universe was fucked, and sent us something akin to Voyager's Golden Record - a last testament to their existence. That detail drove home that they really were just like us (aside from being made of antimatter). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To parrot Thekillerax, I thought this did a lot of things very nicely. Anything that portrays interaction between the Foundation and alternate universes is always fun to see, and the notes from the alternate universe were very good and quite sad. I found it especially bittersweet that they sent a room for their guy as well as advanced waste recycling schematics. The last gifts from a dying universe…

Enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poor little transdimensional rag-and-bone man :( +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1483 is fine (or at least the Foundation didn't add any new horrors to their world, anyway.) There are also a couple others that if anything are far more likely to destroy the Foundation's universe than the other way around, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's not true,
experiment-log-261-ad-de.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is it that every time we make contact with an alternate reality their reality goes to shit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's quite a bit of stuff in here that I like. The Foundation interacting diplomatically with other worlds is always nice, the whole "one man's trash" concept was executed well, and the final Addendum entry was pretty touching. I also especially liked the fact that to him, the Foundationverse was the one made out of antimatter. It's a nice exercise in perspective, above all else. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recall this as well; unfortunately, I feel this version is worse. It doesn't add much that's interesting (I won't go into more detail because I already did in previous iterations of this), but also adds a lot of detail which doesn't add anything to the story, just the reading length.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember the first time this was posted, and you addressed all the problems I had then. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah right, i thought the former version was still up and became confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That last data burst kindof feels like an attempt to 'save' some history and knowledge in light of a major catastrophy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have recieved permission from Crayne to apply tags. (Cheers Crayne!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoah.. There's so much more now compared to when I saw it in drafts. It's looking muuuch better, actually. Good job, Jack. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
95 kg is a 4081 Mt equivalent. I am having trouble finding a calculator that gives me a blast radius for an explosion that big though.

ETA, okay I found one. It looks like you have shock damage out to 60 km in a vaccuum, and need 5mm of aluminum armor to stop the gamma at 100km.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kleenex, better now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would also be one of my only complaints about the article too. It could be cut down a fair degree to keep the most pertinent info the reader needs about 2493.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still don't like that first addendum. There's just… so much of it. I feel you could probably cut it in half without losing anything necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The suit does not have insulating properties, which is why I did not implement the 0 K aspect. In regards to keeping him in a vacuum, that would also have caused issues due to the pressure of anti air within his suit - in a vacuum the suit would have expanded drastically, and may have been at risk of bursting. Likewise, at the time when the containment procedures were in effect, it would have caused -1 severe discomfort, which is the opposite of what the Foundation's intentions were at the time.

Nonetheless, the mentioned containment procedures are no longer used, as shown with the note shown in the collapsible name (when opened). He is now kept within his own specially-made room. I will be updating that the room is a notable distance from Area-32 though. Do you have any suggestions for a reasonable distance to place it at?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After all, the guy is sitting/standing on the floor, and he'll continue the trajectory after the floor is gone.

A trajectory that will take him back to the lunar surface unless he reaches 2.38 km/s before it blows up. Of course that part of the lunar surface might be far enough away.

Of course, an even better idea is to stick him in a room that's far enough away not to effect the rest of the facility. Probably connected via a high-speed light rail. That way there, no need to worry if his suit ruptures.

Yeah, that's a better plan.

ETA: He really would be better off if the home office sent him some duct tape made of buffer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Less bad than letting the whole thing sit there (there will likely be shrapnel at some point if it simply sits there, and that can penetrate the suit, leading to guy-parts hitting the walls, leading to… well, you get the point). After all, the guy is sitting/standing on the floor, and he'll continue the trajectory after the floor is gone.

It's the difference of loosing a part of the facility or the whole damn thing.

Of course, an even better idea is to stick him in a room that's far enough away not to effect the rest of the facility. Probably connected via a high-speed light rail. That way there, no need to worry if his suit ruptures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there's a leak in the suit, high pressure air (I'm not sure if the life support gear on the other side would adjust the pressure down in vaccuum or not; so let's say between 20-100 kPa), the chamber starts filling with anti-air, sensors detect the first interactions with the vessel walls, and automatically launches the chamber. The chamber starts accelerating at greater than 1.62 m/s2, pushing the floor of the chamber into the molecules of anti-air gases, and into the suit, including into the stream of venting atmosphere. Isn't that really bad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but a vacuum means that there's actually time to respond and jettison the unit. Currently, if the suit tears, the whole thing goes into an apocalyptic explosion faster than you can even think about saying "oh, shit". A vacuum might not leave enough time for a human to respond, but an automated system could certainly respond the moment it detects a gas or matter-antimatter interactions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this one! Things seem to have gotten a lot more dire. I love that last addendum, but large portions of the first feel explainy and overall redundant alongside the interview. Still worth an upvote; hoping it sticks this time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Antimatter leaving his suit from his atmosphere leaking is a problem, even in a vaccuum environment. Even a liter of anti-air would be the equivalent of 554 tons of TNT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the event of SCP-2493-2 becoming punctured, it and SCP-2493-1 are to be immediately jettisoned from the facility.

Again, this is not necessary as the chamber is filled with normal helium. The facility will be destroyed before anyone can notice the guy's suit developing a hole and jettison him from the facility. Helium may be largely chemically inert, but matter-antimatter interaction is not a chemical interaction.

Again, keeping him in a vacuum and chilling interior walls to 0k (or as close as possible) should keep anything inside the suit inert long enough for the whole block to be jettisoned. Hell, just keeping him in a vacuum would be good enough if there's concern that his suit can't keep him warm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Deja Vu.

item.title: .roll 20d40
item.content:encoded:
Huge thanks to everyone who helped, (My list is gone am sorreh,) and here is the second third rendition of "One Man's Garbage" (The previous rendition being the 527 before Mr. Fish in the same slot.)
Major Edit (1/2/2016) courtesy of Randomini!
Minor Edit (2/2/2016) courtesy of first 13 comments in discussion.
Major Edit (8/2/2016) courtesy of -9 rating.
Minor Edit (9/2/2016) courtesy of first 13 comments in discussion. (Deja Vu.)

This artwork of SCP-2493 brought to you by AidenEldritch!
